name: SQL & Data
blurb: Analyst-grade SQL, from SELECT to window functions.

lessons.title: The Six Words That Run the Show: SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY & the Logical Order Nobody Tells You
lessons.concept:
Here is the single most powerful idea in all of SQL, and most people learn it years too late: the order you *write* a query is not the order the database *runs* it. You write SELECT first, but the engine reads FROM first. Think of a SQL query as a kitchen. FROM decides which ingredients (tables) come out of the fridge. WHERE throws away the rows you don't want, one row at a time, before anything is grouped or counted. Only after that does SELECT decide which columns to plate up, and ORDER BY arranges them for serving. The actual logical order is: FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY.

Why should an ambitious analyst at a company like Flipkart or HDFC Bank care? Because this one fact explains a whole family of "why is my query broken?" mistakes. It explains why you cannot use a column alias you defined in SELECT inside your WHERE clause — WHERE runs *before* SELECT exists, so the alias hasn't been born yet. It explains why filtering happens before sorting, and why WHERE cannot see aggregate results. Once the logical order lives in your head, SQL stops feeling like magic and starts feeling like a pipeline you control.

WHERE is your row-level filter and it is where 80% of real work happens. Master its operators: `=`, `<>` (not equal), `>`, `<`, `BETWEEN a AND b` (inclusive on both ends), `IN (...)`, `LIKE` for text patterns (`%` = any run of characters, `_` = exactly one character), and `AND`/`OR` with parentheses to control grouping. ORDER BY sorts the final result — `ASC` (default, small to large) or `DESC` (large to small) — and you can sort by multiple columns, each with its own direction. Remember: ORDER BY is the *last* thing that runs, so it is the only clause that *can* use aliases from SELECT.
lessons.example: You run analytics for a Zomato-style food delivery app in Bengaluru. You have an `orders` table with columns `order_id`, `city`, `amount`, `status`, `ordered_at`. Your manager asks: "Show me the 10 biggest completed orders in Bengaluru, largest first." That single business question maps cleanly onto FROM (orders) → WHERE (city and status filters) → ORDER BY (amount descending) → LIMIT (top 10).
lessons.code:
-- Top 10 completed Bengaluru orders, biggest first
SELECT order_id,
       amount,
       ordered_at
FROM orders
WHERE city = 'Bengaluru'
  AND status = 'COMPLETED'
  AND amount BETWEEN 200 AND 5000   -- inclusive of 200 and 5000
ORDER BY amount DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Logical execution order the engine actually uses:
-- 1. FROM orders          (get the rows)
-- 2. WHERE city/status... (throw away rows that don't match)
-- 3. SELECT order_id...   (keep only these columns)
-- 4. ORDER BY amount DESC (sort what survived)
-- 5. LIMIT 10             (keep the first 10)
lessons.tryit: Take any table you can access (or imagine an `employees` table with `name`, `dept`, `salary`, `city`). Write one query that returns names and salaries of employees in Gurugram earning over ₹8,00,000, sorted by salary from highest to lowest. Then deliberately try to use a SELECT alias inside WHERE and watch it fail — that error is the logical order teaching you personally.

mistakes.m: Using a column alias defined in SELECT inside the WHERE clause, e.g. `SELECT amount*1.18 AS total ... WHERE total > 1000`.
mistakes.fix: WHERE runs before SELECT, so the alias doesn't exist yet. Repeat the expression in WHERE (`WHERE amount*1.18 > 1000`) or wrap the query in a subquery/CTE and filter on the outer level.

mistakes.m: Assuming `BETWEEN 200 AND 5000` excludes the endpoints, or that string comparisons are case-insensitive everywhere.
mistakes.fix: BETWEEN is inclusive on both ends (it means `>= 200 AND <= 5000`). Case sensitivity depends on the database and its collation — Postgres is case-sensitive by default, MySQL often isn't. When in doubt, normalise with `LOWER(city) = 'bengaluru'`.

mistakes.m: Believing rows come back in a predictable order without ORDER BY ("they looked sorted last time").
mistakes.fix: Without ORDER BY, SQL makes NO guarantee about row order — it can change between runs, indexes, or engine versions. If order matters, always state it explicitly with ORDER BY.

links.t: PostgreSQL: The SELECT statement and clause order
SQL basics tutorial (W3Schools-style reference on MDN's SQL overview) — Wikipedia: SQL

quiz.q: In a query with FROM, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, SELECT and ORDER BY, which clause does the database evaluate FIRST?
opts: SELECT
FROM
WHERE
ORDER BY
quiz.why: The engine must first know which table(s) to pull rows from before it can filter, group, or select anything. FROM establishes the source rows; everything else operates on that result. This is why FROM sits at the top of the logical execution order even though we write SELECT first.
quiz.approach: Memorise the logical order FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY. It explains most 'why won't this run?' errors.

quiz.q: Why does `SELECT price*1.18 AS gst_total FROM items WHERE gst_total > 500` throw an error?
opts: Because you cannot multiply in a SELECT clause
Because WHERE runs before SELECT, so the alias gst_total does not exist yet when WHERE is evaluated
Because aliases must be in double quotes
Because 1.18 is not an integer
quiz.why: WHERE is evaluated before SELECT in the logical order, so the alias created in SELECT hasn't come into existence when WHERE runs. The fix is to repeat the expression (`WHERE price*1.18 > 500`) or wrap it in a subquery/CTE. Only ORDER BY, which runs after SELECT, can use SELECT aliases.
quiz.approach: If you need an alias in a filter, either repeat the expression or push the calculation into a CTE/subquery and filter on the outer query.

quiz.q: `WHERE amount BETWEEN 200 AND 5000` matches an order of exactly ₹5000. True or false, and why?
opts: False — BETWEEN excludes the upper bound
True — BETWEEN is inclusive of both endpoints
False — BETWEEN only works on dates
True — but only because 5000 is a round number
quiz.why: BETWEEN a AND b is inclusive on both sides, equivalent to `>= a AND <= b`. So an amount of exactly 200 or exactly 5000 both match. Misremembering this off-by-one is a classic source of subtle reporting bugs.
quiz.approach: When boundaries matter (money, dates), mentally expand BETWEEN into its `>=` and `<=` form so you never miscount the edges.

lessons.title: From Rows to Insight: GROUP BY, Aggregates & the HAVING vs WHERE Divide
lessons.concept:
A raw table is a pile of individual facts: this order was ₹450, that one ₹1,200. Business questions almost never ask about single rows — they ask about *groups*: "What's the average order value per city?", "How many loans did each branch disburse?", "Total GST collected per month?" GROUP BY is the tool that collapses many rows into one summary row per group. You name the column(s) to group by, and then every other column in your SELECT must either be one of those grouping columns or be wrapped in an aggregate function — COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX. That rule feels annoying at first, but it's logical: if you group 10,000 Mumbai orders into a single 'Mumbai' row, which single `amount` would the database print? It can't pick one, so you must tell it how to combine them (SUM? AVG?).

Now the distinction that separates beginners from professionals: WHERE vs HAVING. Both filter, but they filter at *different stages*. WHERE filters individual rows *before* grouping — it cannot see aggregates because the groups don't exist yet. HAVING filters *groups* after aggregation — it's the only place you can write conditions on SUM, COUNT, AVG. So "only include orders above ₹100" is a WHERE (row-level). "Only show cities whose total sales exceed ₹10 lakh" is a HAVING (group-level). Getting this right, and in the right order, is the mark of someone who actually understands the engine.

Two COUNT subtleties worth burning into memory. `COUNT(*)` counts rows, including rows with NULLs. `COUNT(column)` counts only rows where that column is NOT NULL. And `COUNT(DISTINCT column)` counts unique non-null values. These three give different numbers, and choosing the wrong one silently corrupts a report. For example, in a customer table `COUNT(*)` might be 50,000 (total rows) while `COUNT(DISTINCT pan_number)` is 48,200 (unique customers) — the gap reveals duplicates.
lessons.example: You're a BI analyst at an NBFC. Leadership wants a branch performance view: for each branch, how many loans were disbursed and the total disbursed amount — but only counting approved loans, and only showing branches that disbursed more than ₹50 lakh in total. This needs a WHERE (approved loans only, row-level) AND a HAVING (total over ₹50 lakh, group-level) in the same query — the perfect illustration of the divide.
lessons.code:
SELECT branch_name,
       COUNT(*)          AS approved_loans,
       SUM(amount)       AS total_disbursed,
       AVG(amount)       AS avg_loan_size
FROM loans
WHERE status = 'APPROVED'          -- row filter: BEFORE grouping
GROUP BY branch_name
HAVING SUM(amount) > 5000000       -- group filter: AFTER aggregation (₹50 lakh)
ORDER BY total_disbursed DESC;

-- WHERE removes non-approved loans row by row.
-- GROUP BY collapses remaining rows into one per branch.
-- HAVING then drops whole branches whose SUM is <= 50 lakh.
lessons.tryit: On an `orders(city, amount, status)` dataset, write a query that shows each city, its order count, and its average order value — for completed orders only — keeping only cities with at least 100 completed orders. You must use WHERE for `status = 'COMPLETED'` and HAVING for the count threshold. Then swap them deliberately and observe the error, so the difference is unforgettable.

mistakes.m: Putting an aggregate condition in WHERE, e.g. `WHERE SUM(amount) > 5000000`.
mistakes.fix: WHERE runs before aggregation, so it cannot see SUM/COUNT/AVG. Any condition on an aggregate must go in HAVING, which runs after GROUP BY.

mistakes.m: Selecting a column that is neither grouped nor aggregated, e.g. `SELECT branch_name, customer_name, SUM(amount) ... GROUP BY branch_name`.
mistakes.fix: Every non-aggregated column in SELECT must appear in GROUP BY. Either add `customer_name` to GROUP BY (changing the grouping level) or wrap it in an aggregate like `MAX(customer_name)` — or, more likely, remove it because it doesn't belong at branch level.

mistakes.m: Using COUNT(column) when you meant to count all rows, and getting a lower number because that column has NULLs.
mistakes.fix: Use COUNT(*) to count rows regardless of NULLs, COUNT(column) to count non-null values only, and COUNT(DISTINCT column) for unique values. Pick deliberately based on the question.

links.t: Mode SQL Tutorial: GROUP BY and HAVING

quiz.q: You want only cities whose TOTAL sales exceed ₹10 lakh. Where does that condition belong?
opts: In WHERE, because it's a filter
In HAVING, because it filters on an aggregate (SUM) computed per group
In ORDER BY
In the SELECT list
quiz.why: SUM(sales) is an aggregate that only exists after GROUP BY collapses rows into groups. WHERE runs before grouping and can't see aggregates, so a condition on a group total must go in HAVING, which runs after aggregation. This is the defining WHERE-vs-HAVING distinction.
quiz.approach: Ask: is my condition about a single row, or about a whole group's aggregate? Row → WHERE. Aggregate → HAVING.

quiz.q: A customers table has 50,000 rows. `COUNT(*)` = 50000 but `COUNT(email)` = 47,300. What does the gap mean?
opts: The query is wrong
2,700 rows have a NULL email, since COUNT(column) skips NULLs
There are 2,700 duplicate emails
email is not indexed
quiz.why: COUNT(*) counts every row, while COUNT(email) counts only rows where email is NOT NULL. A difference of 2,700 means 2,700 rows have a NULL email. (Duplicates would be revealed by comparing COUNT(email) with COUNT(DISTINCT email), a different check.)
quiz.approach: Use COUNT(*) vs COUNT(col) vs COUNT(DISTINCT col) deliberately — each answers a different question about rows, non-nulls, and uniqueness.

quiz.q: Why does `SELECT branch, customer_name, SUM(amount) FROM loans GROUP BY branch` fail in standard SQL?
opts: SUM cannot be used with GROUP BY
customer_name is neither in GROUP BY nor wrapped in an aggregate, so the engine can't reduce it to one value per branch
You cannot group by a text column
GROUP BY must come before FROM
quiz.why: When you group by branch, each branch becomes one row, but a branch has many different customer_name values. Standard SQL requires every non-aggregated SELECT column to appear in GROUP BY, so the engine knows exactly one value to show. Either add customer_name to GROUP BY or aggregate it.
quiz.approach: Rule of thumb: in a GROUP BY query, every SELECTed column is either a grouping key or lives inside an aggregate function. No exceptions in standard SQL.

lessons.title: JOINs Demystified: INNER, LEFT, RIGHT, FULL — and the LEFT-JOIN Filter Trap That Ruins Reports
lessons.concept:
Real data lives in many tables — one for customers, one for orders, one for products — connected by keys. JOINs stitch them back together. The mental model: a JOIN matches rows from two tables using an ON condition, usually `a.id = b.foreign_id`. An INNER JOIN keeps only rows that match on *both* sides — customers who have orders, and orders that have a valid customer. A LEFT JOIN keeps *every* row from the left table, and attaches matching right-table rows where they exist; where there's no match, the right-side columns come back as NULL. A RIGHT JOIN is the mirror image (every right row kept). A FULL OUTER JOIN keeps everything from both sides, filling NULLs wherever there's no match. Ninety percent of the time you'll reach for INNER or LEFT.

The LEFT JOIN exists precisely to answer questions like "which customers have placed *zero* orders?" — you keep all customers, and the ones with no order show NULL in the order columns. This is where the single most infamous trap in SQL lives, and it catches even experienced analysts. If you write a LEFT JOIN to keep all customers, but then put a condition on the *right* (order) table in your WHERE clause — say `WHERE orders.status = 'COMPLETED'` — you have secretly destroyed the LEFT JOIN. Why? Because for a customer with no orders, `orders.status` is NULL, and `NULL = 'COMPLETED'` is not true, so WHERE throws that customer's row away. Your "keep everyone" LEFT JOIN silently becomes an INNER JOIN. The report that was supposed to show inactive customers now hides exactly the customers you were looking for.

The fix is precise: conditions that should *not* eliminate unmatched rows belong in the ON clause, not the WHERE clause. `LEFT JOIN orders ON c.id = o.customer_id AND o.status = 'COMPLETED'` filters *which orders get attached* while still keeping every customer. Conditions on the *left* table are safe in WHERE. Conditions on the right table that must preserve non-matches go in ON. Internalise this and you'll debug a class of bugs that quietly corrupts dashboards across the industry.
lessons.example: You run retention analytics for a fintech app. Product asks: "How many registered users made zero transactions this quarter?" You LEFT JOIN users to transactions. If you carelessly add `WHERE transactions.amount > 0` in the WHERE clause, every zero-transaction user (the exact people you're counting) gets NULL for amount, fails the filter, and vanishes — you'd report zero, which is catastrophically wrong. Moving that logic into ON preserves them.
lessons.code:
-- WRONG: the WHERE turns the LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(t.txn_id) AS txns
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN transactions t ON u.user_id = t.user_id
WHERE t.txn_date >= '2026-04-01'        -- kills users with no txns!
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name;

-- RIGHT: the date condition moves into ON, so unmatched users survive
SELECT u.user_id, u.name, COUNT(t.txn_id) AS txns
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN transactions t
       ON u.user_id = t.user_id
      AND t.txn_date >= '2026-04-01'
GROUP BY u.user_id, u.name
HAVING COUNT(t.txn_id) = 0;             -- exactly the zero-txn users
lessons.tryit: Build two tiny tables: `customers(id, name)` with 5 rows and `orders(id, customer_id, status)` where only 3 customers have orders. Write a LEFT JOIN that lists ALL 5 customers with their order count. Then add `WHERE orders.status = 'COMPLETED'` in the WHERE clause and watch your row count drop — you just reproduced the trap. Finally, move that condition into ON and confirm all 5 customers return.

mistakes.m: Putting a right-table filter in WHERE on a LEFT JOIN, silently converting it into an INNER JOIN and dropping the unmatched rows.
mistakes.fix: If a condition on the right table must NOT eliminate non-matching left rows, put it in the ON clause. Keep only left-table conditions (or an explicit `IS NULL` check) in WHERE.

mistakes.m: Thinking INNER vs LEFT is just style — 'they usually give the same rows anyway'.
mistakes.fix: They give the same rows only when every left row has a match. The moment unmatched rows exist (inactive customers, unsold products), INNER silently hides them. Choose based on whether you need the unmatched rows.

mistakes.m: Forgetting the ON condition entirely (or writing a comma join), producing a Cartesian product — every row times every row.
mistakes.fix: Always specify an explicit ON with the correct key. A join of 10,000 x 10,000 rows with no ON returns 100 million rows — a classic way to hang a database and blow up a report.

quiz.q: You LEFT JOIN customers to orders to find customers with zero orders, then add `WHERE orders.status = 'COMPLETED'`. What happens?
opts: It works correctly
The LEFT JOIN effectively becomes an INNER JOIN, dropping the zero-order customers you were trying to find
It returns a Cartesian product
It errors because status is a string
quiz.why: For a customer with no orders, orders.status is NULL. The comparison `NULL = 'COMPLETED'` is not TRUE, so WHERE discards that row — exactly the customers you needed. The right-table condition in WHERE silently converts the LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN. The fix is to move the condition into the ON clause.
quiz.approach: Right-table filters that must preserve unmatched left rows belong in ON, not WHERE. This is the number-one LEFT JOIN bug.

quiz.q: An INNER JOIN and a LEFT JOIN between the same two tables return identical rows. What must be true?
opts: The tables are identical
Every row in the left table has at least one match in the right table
The join has no ON clause
One table is empty
quiz.why: A LEFT JOIN adds, over an INNER JOIN, exactly the left rows that have NO match (with NULLs on the right). If the two return the same rows, there are no unmatched left rows — every left row matched. The difference between the two join types only appears when unmatched left rows exist.
quiz.approach: Reach for LEFT JOIN when the unmatched rows are part of the answer (inactive users, unsold SKUs); INNER when you only want matched pairs.

quiz.q: A JOIN with no ON condition between a 5,000-row and a 4,000-row table returns how many rows?
opts: 20,000,000 (a Cartesian product: 5,000 × 4,000)
quiz.why: Without a join condition, SQL pairs every left row with every right row — a Cartesian product of 5,000 × 4,000 = 20 million rows. This is almost never intended and can overwhelm the database. Always specify an explicit ON with the correct key.
quiz.approach: Every intentional join needs an ON clause on the right key. If you truly want all combinations, use an explicit CROSS JOIN so the intent is clear.

lessons.title: NULL: The Silent Saboteur of Every Report
lessons.concept:
NULL is not zero. NULL is not an empty string. NULL means 'unknown' or 'missing' — the value exists in reality but the database doesn't know it, or it genuinely has no value. This distinction is the source of more silent reporting errors than almost anything else in SQL, because NULL follows a different logic — three-valued logic (TRUE, FALSE, and UNKNOWN). The golden rule: you cannot compare anything to NULL with `=`. `salary = NULL` does not return TRUE even for a row where salary is NULL; it returns UNKNOWN, which WHERE treats as 'not true', so the row is excluded. To test for NULL you must use `IS NULL` or `IS NOT NULL`. Write `WHERE manager_id IS NULL`, never `WHERE manager_id = NULL`.

NULL also quietly poisons arithmetic and aggregates. Any arithmetic with NULL yields NULL: `1000 + NULL` is NULL, not 1000. So if a `bonus` column is NULL for some employees, `salary + bonus` becomes NULL for exactly those employees, and they may drop out of downstream filters. Aggregates behave subtly: SUM and AVG *ignore* NULLs rather than treating them as zero. This matters enormously for AVG. If 100 employees have a bonus but 50 have NULL bonus, `AVG(bonus)` divides the total by 100 (the non-null count), not 150. If you meant 'average across all 150 employees, treating missing as zero', you'll overstate the average. The tool to control this is COALESCE(value, fallback), which returns the first non-null argument: `COALESCE(bonus, 0)` turns NULLs into zeros before the math.

Two more traps that catch professionals. First, `NOT IN` with a subquery that contains even one NULL returns no rows at all — because `x NOT IN (1, 2, NULL)` evaluates to UNKNOWN, never TRUE. Prefer `NOT EXISTS`, which is NULL-safe. Second, NULLs cluster at one end when sorting, and different databases disagree on where: PostgreSQL puts NULLs last by default in ascending order, while some engines put them first — so use `ORDER BY col NULLS LAST` (or `NULLS FIRST`) when it matters. NULL handling is precisely the kind of unglamorous rigor that separates a report you can trust from one that quietly lies.
lessons.example: You compute average bonus across your Gurugram team of 150. Fifty employees joined after the bonus cycle and have NULL bonus. Your manager reads `AVG(bonus)` as ₹1,20,000 and budgets accordingly — but that average was computed over only the 100 employees who *had* a bonus. The true average across all 150 (treating new joiners as ₹0) is just ₹80,000. That ₹40,000-per-head gap, multiplied across the team, is a budgeting error caused entirely by misunderstanding how AVG treats NULL.
lessons.code:
-- The two averages tell very different stories
SELECT
  AVG(bonus)                 AS avg_among_those_who_have_one,  -- ignores 50 NULLs
  AVG(COALESCE(bonus, 0))    AS avg_across_everyone,           -- NULLs count as 0
  COUNT(*)                   AS headcount,      -- 150 (all rows)
  COUNT(bonus)               AS with_bonus      -- 100 (non-null only)
FROM employees
WHERE city = 'Gurugram';

-- Testing for NULL correctly:
SELECT name FROM employees WHERE manager_id IS NULL;   -- top of hierarchy
-- WRONG: WHERE manager_id = NULL   -> returns nothing, silently
lessons.tryit: Create an `employees(name, salary, bonus)` table where 3 of 8 rows have NULL bonus. Run `SELECT AVG(bonus)` and `SELECT AVG(COALESCE(bonus,0))` side by side and explain the difference in one sentence. Then write a query using `IS NULL` to list exactly the employees missing a bonus — and confirm `= NULL` returns nothing.

mistakes.m: Testing for missing values with `= NULL` or `<> NULL`.
mistakes.fix: Comparisons with NULL always yield UNKNOWN, so these match nothing. Use `IS NULL` and `IS NOT NULL` — they are the only NULL-safe tests.

mistakes.m: Assuming AVG treats NULLs as zero, or that `SUM(col)` includes missing rows as 0.
mistakes.fix: SUM and AVG ignore NULLs entirely — AVG divides by the count of non-null values only. If missing should count as zero, wrap with COALESCE(col, 0) before aggregating.

mistakes.m: Using `NOT IN (subquery)` when the subquery can contain a NULL, then getting zero rows and no error.
mistakes.fix: A single NULL in the list makes `NOT IN` return UNKNOWN for every row, so nothing matches. Use `NOT EXISTS` instead, which handles NULLs correctly, or filter NULLs out of the subquery.

links.t: PostgreSQL: Comparison operators and NULL handling
Mode SQL Tutorial: Working with NULLs

quiz.q: Which query correctly finds employees who have no manager assigned (manager_id is missing)?
opts: WHERE manager_id = NULL
WHERE manager_id IS NULL
WHERE manager_id <> 0
WHERE manager_id = ''
quiz.why: NULL means 'unknown', and any comparison to it using `=` returns UNKNOWN, not TRUE — so `manager_id = NULL` matches no rows even where the value is genuinely NULL. The only NULL-safe test is `IS NULL`. Note `<> 0` and `= ''` also fail because NULL is neither zero nor an empty string.
quiz.approach: Reach for IS NULL / IS NOT NULL every time you test for missing data. Never use = or <> against NULL.

quiz.q: A column `bonus` is NULL for 50 of 150 employees. `AVG(bonus)` returns ₹1,20,000. What does this figure actually represent?
opts: The average over all 150 employees
The average over only the 100 employees who have a non-null bonus
Always an error
The sum divided by 150
quiz.why: AVG ignores NULLs — it sums the non-null bonuses and divides by the count of non-null values (100), not the full headcount (150). To get an average across everyone treating missing as zero, use `AVG(COALESCE(bonus, 0))`, which would divide by 150 and give a lower number.
quiz.approach: Decide explicitly whether 'missing' means 'exclude' (raw AVG) or 'count as zero' (COALESCE first). The two answers can differ enormously.

quiz.q: `SELECT name FROM staff WHERE dept_id NOT IN (SELECT dept_id FROM closed_depts)` returns zero rows unexpectedly. The likely cause?
opts: staff is empty
The closed_depts subquery contains a NULL dept_id, making NOT IN evaluate to UNKNOWN for every row
NOT IN is invalid SQL
dept_id is a string
quiz.why: If the subquery yields any NULL, then `dept_id NOT IN (..., NULL)` becomes UNKNOWN for every row (since the engine can't confirm dept_id differs from an unknown value), so nothing is returned. This is a notorious silent bug. `NOT EXISTS` is NULL-safe and is the recommended fix.
quiz.approach: Prefer NOT EXISTS over NOT IN whenever the subquery might contain NULLs — it sidesteps three-valued-logic surprises entirely.

lessons.title: The Order SQL Really Runs: WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING & the NULL Traps
lessons.concept:
You read a query top-to-bottom starting with SELECT, but the database does not run it that way. The engine's logical order is FROM -> WHERE -> GROUP BY -> HAVING -> SELECT -> ORDER BY -> LIMIT. Picture an assembly line: FROM pulls the rows, WHERE throws out individual rows, GROUP BY collapses survivors into buckets, HAVING throws out whole buckets, SELECT finally computes your output columns, ORDER BY sorts, and LIMIT trims. This one mental model explains the vast majority of 'why won't my query work' errors.

Two consequences fall straight out of that order. First, a column alias you invent in SELECT cannot be used in WHERE, because WHERE ran before SELECT even existed. ORDER BY, which runs last, CAN reuse that alias. Second, WHERE versus HAVING: WHERE filters raw rows before aggregation and literally cannot see an aggregate like SUM(); HAVING filters buckets after aggregation and is exactly where aggregate conditions belong. Filtering early in WHERE is also faster, because fewer rows ever reach the expensive grouping step.

Then there is NULL, the single biggest source of silent wrong answers. NULL means 'unknown' -- not zero, not empty string. SQL uses three-valued logic, so NULL = NULL is not TRUE, it is UNKNOWN, and WHERE keeps only rows that are TRUE. That is why you must write IS NULL / IS NOT NULL. Aggregates quietly skip NULLs too: COUNT(col) ignores NULLs while COUNT(*) counts every row, and AVG(amount) divides by the count of non-null amounts, not the total rows -- a classic reason an average silently looks too high.
lessons.example: At Cars24, a listings table has (city, price, sold_date, inspection_score) where sold_date is NULL for cars still on the lot. To find cities whose SOLD cars average above 5 lakh, you filter rows first (WHERE sold_date IS NOT NULL), collapse by city (GROUP BY city), then filter buckets (HAVING AVG(price) > 500000). If you instead wrote WHERE price = NULL you would get zero rows, because equality with NULL is never TRUE.
lessons.code:
SELECT city,
       COUNT(*)   AS cars_listed,
       AVG(price) AS avg_price
FROM listings
WHERE sold_date IS NOT NULL      -- row filter, runs first
GROUP BY city
HAVING AVG(price) > 500000        -- group filter, runs after aggregation
ORDER BY avg_price DESC;          -- ORDER BY can use the alias avg_price
lessons.tryit: On any table with a nullable column, run SELECT COUNT(*), COUNT(that_column) in one query and confirm the gap equals the number of NULLs. Then add WHERE that_column IS NOT NULL and watch the two counts converge.

mistakes.m: Using a SELECT alias in WHERE, e.g. SELECT price*0.18 AS gst ... WHERE gst > 100.
mistakes.fix: WHERE runs before SELECT, so the alias does not exist yet. Repeat the expression in WHERE, or wrap the query in a subquery/CTE and filter on the alias in the outer query.

mistakes.m: Putting an aggregate condition in WHERE, like WHERE SUM(price) > 100000.
mistakes.fix: WHERE cannot see aggregates because it runs before GROUP BY. Aggregate filters belong in HAVING.

mistakes.m: Writing col = NULL or col != NULL to test for missing values.
mistakes.fix: Any comparison with NULL yields UNKNOWN, so the row is dropped. Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL instead.

links.t: Mode: The SQL Tutorial for Data Analysis

quiz.q: In the logical processing order, which clause filters individual rows BEFORE any grouping happens?
opts: WHERE
HAVING
SELECT
ORDER BY
quiz.why: WHERE runs right after FROM and before GROUP BY, so it filters raw rows. HAVING only runs after aggregation and filters whole groups. Filtering in WHERE also means fewer rows reach the grouping step.
quiz.approach: Filter as early as possible in WHERE; reserve HAVING for conditions that depend on aggregates.

quiz.q: A query does SELECT price*0.18 AS gst ... WHERE gst > 100 and errors out. Why?
opts: You cannot multiply inside SELECT
The alias gst is not available in WHERE because WHERE is evaluated before SELECT
All conditions must move to HAVING
Aliases must be written in uppercase
quiz.why: SELECT is where the alias is born, and it runs after WHERE in the logical order, so the alias is invisible to WHERE. You must repeat price*0.18 in WHERE or use a subquery/CTE.
quiz.approach: Remember ORDER BY is the only clause that can reuse a SELECT alias.

quiz.q: A column phone has 30 NULLs out of 100 rows. What does COUNT(phone) return?
quiz.why: COUNT(column) ignores NULLs and counts only non-null values, so it returns 70. COUNT(*) would return 100 because it counts rows regardless of NULLs.
quiz.approach: Use COUNT(*) for row counts and COUNT(col) when you specifically want non-null values.

lessons.title: Joining Without Losing Rows: JOIN Types, the LEFT-JOIN Filter Trap & UNION vs UNION ALL
lessons.concept:
A join stitches rows from two tables together using a matching condition. INNER JOIN keeps only rows that match on both sides. LEFT JOIN keeps every row from the left table and attaches matching right-table columns, filling NULL wherever there is no match (RIGHT JOIN is its mirror; FULL OUTER keeps unmatched rows from both sides; CROSS JOIN pairs every row with every row). In analytics, LEFT JOIN is your default whenever you want to keep a complete 'spine' -- all customers, all dates -- even the ones with no activity. This is precisely how a star schema is queried: a central fact table (orders, transactions) joins outward to dimension tables (customer, product, date) on foreign keys.

Now the most expensive beginner bug in all of SQL: the LEFT-JOIN filter trap. You LEFT JOIN customers to orders to keep every customer, then casually add WHERE orders.status = 'delivered'. Silently your LEFT JOIN now behaves like an INNER JOIN. Why? For a customer with no orders, orders.status is NULL, and NULL = 'delivered' is UNKNOWN, so WHERE discards that customer. The fix is to move the right-table condition into the ON clause: LEFT JOIN orders ON orders.customer_id = c.id AND orders.status = 'delivered'. Conditions in ON limit what gets matched during the join; conditions in WHERE filter the finished result -- and filtering NULLs there destroys your outer rows.

To stack rows on top of each other (not columns side by side) use UNION or UNION ALL. Both require the same number of columns with compatible types, matched by position, not by name. UNION ALL returns everything including duplicates and is fast. UNION additionally removes duplicate rows, which forces the database into a sort or hash pass over the whole result, so it is slower. The rule of thumb: default to UNION ALL and only reach for UNION when you genuinely need de-duplication.
lessons.example: On a PhonePe-style schema, customers is the spine and transactions is the fact table. To count DELIVERED-equivalent successful transactions per customer while still listing customers with zero, you LEFT JOIN and place the status filter in ON, not WHERE. Separately, to build one report from txn_jun and txn_jul, you use UNION ALL -- the two archives are already distinct, so paying the de-dup cost of UNION would be pure waste.
lessons.code:
-- Keep ALL customers; count only successful txns
SELECT c.customer_id, c.name,
       COUNT(t.txn_id) AS success_txns
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN transactions t
       ON t.customer_id = c.customer_id
      AND t.status = 'SUCCESS'   -- condition in ON preserves left rows
GROUP BY c.customer_id, c.name;

-- Stack two months without paying the de-dup cost
SELECT * FROM txn_jun
UNION ALL
SELECT * FROM txn_jul;
lessons.tryit: Take a customers-orders pair. Write the query with the status filter in WHERE and note the customer count. Move the identical filter into the ON clause and re-run: the count should jump back up as the zero-order customers reappear.

mistakes.m: Adding a right-table filter (e.g. o.status = 'delivered') in WHERE after a LEFT JOIN and wondering why rows vanished.
mistakes.fix: Unmatched rows have NULL on the right side and fail the WHERE test, collapsing your LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN. Move right-table conditions into the ON clause.

mistakes.m: Reaching for UNION everywhere 'to be safe'.
mistakes.fix: UNION silently removes duplicates and pays a sort/hash cost. If rows are already distinct or duplicates are fine, use the faster UNION ALL.

mistakes.m: Assuming COUNT(*) after a LEFT JOIN counts only matched rows.
mistakes.fix: COUNT(*) counts every joined row including the NULL-filled unmatched ones. Use COUNT(right_table_column) to count only genuine matches.

quiz.q: You LEFT JOIN customers to orders to keep all customers, then add WHERE orders.status = 'delivered'. What happens?
opts: Customers with no delivered order are dropped, effectively turning it into an inner join
Nothing changes; a LEFT JOIN protects all left rows regardless of WHERE
It throws a syntax error
Duplicate customers appear
quiz.why: Unmatched customers get NULL for orders.status, and NULL = 'delivered' is UNKNOWN, so WHERE removes them. The LEFT JOIN silently behaves like an INNER JOIN. Putting the condition in ON fixes it.
quiz.approach: Right-table filters that must not drop left rows belong in the ON clause, not WHERE.

quiz.q: What is the key difference between UNION and UNION ALL?
opts: UNION ALL removes duplicates; UNION keeps them
UNION removes duplicate rows (sorting/hashing to do so); UNION ALL keeps everything and is faster
UNION works on only two tables; UNION ALL on many
They are identical in every case
quiz.why: UNION de-duplicates, which requires an extra sort or hash pass over the combined result. UNION ALL simply concatenates rows and is faster.
quiz.approach: Default to UNION ALL and only use UNION when duplicate removal is genuinely required.

quiz.q: In a star schema, the central table holding measures like amount and quantity plus foreign keys to descriptive tables is the:
opts: Dimension table
Fact table
Bridge table
Lookup table
quiz.why: The fact table stores quantitative measures at a defined grain and carries foreign keys out to dimension tables such as customer, product, and date. Descriptive attributes live in the dimensions.
quiz.approach: Model numeric measures in the fact table and descriptive context in dimension tables.

lessons.title: Think in Steps: CTEs, Subqueries & EXISTS (and the NOT IN NULL Landmine)
lessons.concept:
A subquery is simply a query nested inside another. A CTE (Common Table Expression), written WITH name AS (...), is a named subquery you define up front and then use like a temporary table. CTEs are the antidote to unreadable nested queries: they let you lay out a pipeline of named steps -- filter here, aggregate there, join at the end -- and you can reference the same CTE more than once. Treat them primarily as a readability and maintainability tool. (A subtle engine note: in some databases a CTE used to be an optimization boundary that forced materialization, but in modern PostgreSQL 12+ simple non-recursive CTEs are inlined automatically, so you rarely pay a penalty.)

Correlated subqueries and EXISTS are how you ask existence questions. A correlated subquery references the outer row, so it is conceptually re-evaluated for each outer row. EXISTS returns TRUE the instant the inner query finds one matching row and then stops -- it cares only about existence, not values -- which makes it efficient for questions like 'customers who placed at least one order'. Use EXISTS / NOT EXISTS for pure presence or absence checks, and prefer a JOIN when you actually need columns from the other table.

Here is the landmine: NOT IN combined with a subquery that can return NULL. If the inner result contains even a single NULL, NOT IN evaluates to UNKNOWN for every outer row, and the query returns zero rows -- a silent, data-dependent failure that passes in your test data and breaks in production the day one NULL sneaks in. NOT EXISTS does not suffer this, because it works on TRUE/FALSE existence rather than value equality. The rule: for 'not in another set', use NOT EXISTS (or add IS NOT NULL inside the subquery).
lessons.example: At Zomato you want restaurants that received NO orders this month. Written as NOT IN (SELECT restaurant_id FROM orders ...), a single order row with a NULL restaurant_id makes the whole result collapse to zero rows. Rewritten with NOT EXISTS, it stays correct no matter what NULLs exist in the orders table.
lessons.code:
-- Readable pipeline with a CTE
WITH monthly_orders AS (
    SELECT restaurant_id, COUNT(*) AS orders_cnt
    FROM orders
    WHERE order_date >= DATE '2026-07-01'
    GROUP BY restaurant_id
)
SELECT r.name, COALESCE(m.orders_cnt, 0) AS orders_cnt
FROM restaurants r
LEFT JOIN monthly_orders m ON m.restaurant_id = r.id;

-- Safe 'never ordered' check
SELECT r.name
FROM restaurants r
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
    SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.restaurant_id = r.id
);
lessons.tryit: Write the 'restaurants with no orders' query twice -- once with NOT IN and once with NOT EXISTS. Then insert one order row with a NULL restaurant_id and re-run both: watch NOT IN return nothing while NOT EXISTS stays correct.

mistakes.m: Using NOT IN (subquery) where the subquery column can contain NULL.
mistakes.fix: A single NULL forces NOT IN to return no rows. Use NOT EXISTS, or add WHERE col IS NOT NULL inside the subquery.

mistakes.m: Believing a CTE is always materialized into a temp table and therefore slower.
mistakes.fix: Modern optimizers (e.g. PostgreSQL 12+) inline simple CTEs. Treat CTEs as a readability tool and only check the query plan when performance is critical.

mistakes.m: Worrying about which columns to select inside EXISTS.
mistakes.fix: EXISTS only checks whether any row exists, so the select list is irrelevant. SELECT 1 is the idiomatic, clear choice.

links.t: Mode: The SQL Tutorial for Data Analysis

quiz.q: Why can NOT IN (SELECT ...) suddenly return zero rows in production?
opts: Because the subquery is too slow
Because a NULL in the subquery result makes the NOT IN comparison UNKNOWN for every row
Because NOT IN only works on numeric columns
Because NOT IN requires a CTE
quiz.why: NOT IN expands into a chain of inequality comparisons; any NULL in the list yields UNKNOWN, so no row passes and you get an empty result. NOT EXISTS sidesteps this entirely.
quiz.approach: Prefer NOT EXISTS for anti-joins, or guarantee the subquery returns no NULLs.

quiz.q: What does EXISTS actually evaluate?
opts: The sum of the subquery's rows
Whether the subquery returns at least one row (TRUE/FALSE), stopping at the first match
The value of the first selected column
The number of NULLs in the subquery
quiz.why: EXISTS is a boolean presence test. It short-circuits the moment one matching row is found and ignores the select list entirely, which is why SELECT 1 is idiomatic.
quiz.approach: Use EXISTS for presence checks and reserve JOINs for when you need the other table's columns.

quiz.q: In modern PostgreSQL (12+), a simple non-recursive CTE is best described as:
opts: Always materialized into a temp table, so it should be avoided
A readability tool that the optimizer can inline, usually without a performance penalty
Slower than an equivalent subquery in every case
Usable only once per query
quiz.why: PostgreSQL 12+ inlines simple CTEs, so they are primarily about clarity, and a CTE can be referenced multiple times within a query.
quiz.approach: Write CTEs for readability first, and inspect the query plan only when performance truly matters.

lessons.title: Window Functions: Ranking, LAG/LEAD, Running Totals & Cohorts
lessons.concept:
GROUP BY collapses many rows into one. Window functions do the opposite: they compute across a set of related rows -- the 'window' -- while keeping every single row visible. The syntax is func() OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...). PARTITION BY splits rows into independent groups (per city, per customer), and ORDER BY orders the rows inside each partition. This is how you answer 'rank each sales agent within their city' or 'show each order's running total for that customer' without writing a clumsy self-join.

The ranking trio differs precisely on ties. ROW_NUMBER() gives every row a unique number (1,2,3,4) even when values tie -- ideal for 'pick exactly one latest row per customer'. RANK() gives tied rows the same rank and then skips (1,1,3). DENSE_RANK() gives tied rows the same rank with no gap (1,1,2). LAG(col) and LEAD(col) reach into the previous or next row of the partition -- perfect for month-over-month growth, computed as this_month minus LAG(this_month).

For running totals you must understand the frame. The moment you add ORDER BY inside OVER, the default frame is RANGE BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW -- and RANGE treats all rows sharing the same ORDER BY value as one peer group, so tied rows all receive the same 'running' total. If you want a strict row-by-row cumulative sum, spell out ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW. This ROWS-versus-RANGE distinction is a favourite interview trap. Pair windows with DATE_TRUNC('month', signup_date) and you get cohort/retention analysis: bucket users by signup month, then track their later activity per cohort.
lessons.example: On a Cars24 sales dashboard from sales(sales_agent, city, sale_date, amount): rank agents within each city by total sales, compute each month's revenue change with LAG, and build a strict running total of daily sales for July -- all without a self-join.
lessons.code:
-- Top agent per city (exactly one, ties broken by amount)
SELECT *
FROM (
  SELECT sales_agent, city, amount,
         ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY city
                            ORDER BY amount DESC) AS rn
  FROM agent_totals
) t
WHERE rn = 1;

-- Month-over-month change with LAG
SELECT month, revenue,
       revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS mom_change
FROM monthly_revenue;

-- Strict row-by-row running total
SELECT sale_date, amount,
       SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date
                        ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING
                                 AND CURRENT ROW) AS running_total
FROM sales;
lessons.tryit: On a small sales table with deliberate ties, put ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), and DENSE_RANK() side by side in one SELECT using the same PARTITION BY and ORDER BY, and eyeball how each handles a tie: 1,2,3,4 vs 1,1,3 vs 1,1,2.

mistakes.m: Using RANK() when you need exactly one row per group (e.g. the single latest order).
mistakes.fix: RANK() gives tied rows the same number, so you can get several 'rank 1' rows. Use ROW_NUMBER() for a guaranteed single winner.

mistakes.m: Trusting the default running-total frame when ORDER BY values tie.
mistakes.fix: The default RANGE frame lumps peer rows together and gives them equal totals. Use ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW for a true row-by-row cumulative sum.

mistakes.m: Putting a window function directly in WHERE, e.g. WHERE ROW_NUMBER() OVER (...) = 1.
mistakes.fix: Window functions are computed in the SELECT step, after WHERE. Wrap the query in a subquery or CTE and filter on the resulting alias.

quiz.q: You need exactly one row -- the latest order -- per customer. Which function guarantees a single winner even on ties?
opts: RANK()
DENSE_RANK()
ROW_NUMBER()
NTILE()
quiz.why: ROW_NUMBER() assigns unique sequential numbers within each partition, so filtering rn = 1 yields exactly one row. RANK() and DENSE_RANK() can assign the same number to tied rows.
quiz.approach: Use ROW_NUMBER() for de-duplication or 'one per group'; use RANK/DENSE_RANK for leaderboards.

quiz.q: For descending scores 100, 100, 90, what does DENSE_RANK() produce?
quiz.why: DENSE_RANK() gives tied rows the same rank with no gap, so the two 100s are both 1 and the 90 is 2. RANK() would produce 1, 1, 3 because it skips after ties.
quiz.approach: Use DENSE_RANK() when you want no gaps in the ranking numbers.

quiz.q: A SUM(...) OVER (ORDER BY d) running total gives surprising equal totals for rows that share the same date d. Most likely cause?
opts: The default RANGE frame groups peer rows (same d) together; use ROWS BETWEEN for a row-by-row total
SUM cannot be used as a window function
You forgot to add PARTITION BY
ORDER BY is not allowed inside OVER
quiz.why: With ORDER BY and no explicit frame, the default is RANGE UNBOUNDED PRECEDING to CURRENT ROW, and RANGE includes every row tied on the ORDER BY value. Switching to ROWS gives a strict cumulative sum.
quiz.approach: Specify ROWS BETWEEN UNBOUNDED PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW for exact running totals.

lessons.title: Window Functions: See the Row and the Whole Crowd at Once
lessons.concept:
Here is the single most important mental shift for advanced SQL: GROUP BY collapses rows, window functions do not. When you write GROUP BY branch, ten transactions from the Andheri branch become one row. Useful, but you lose the individual transactions. A window function keeps every original row and simply attaches an extra column computed by looking at a 'window' of related rows. You get the row AND the crowd around it in the same result set. This is why analysts reach for windows constantly and juniors who only know GROUP BY struggle.

The anatomy is always the same: function() OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...). PARTITION BY is like GROUP BY for the window — it slices the data into independent buckets (say, per city). ORDER BY inside OVER decides the sequence within each bucket, which matters enormously for ranking and running totals. The three ranking functions are the classic interview trap. ROW_NUMBER gives a strict 1,2,3,4 with no ties ever. RANK gives 1,2,2,4 — ties share a rank and it skips numbers. DENSE_RANK gives 1,2,2,3 — ties share but it never skips. If two SIPs of ₹5,000 tie for second-highest, RANK jumps to 4 next, DENSE_RANK continues at 3.

Then there are the navigation functions LAG and LEAD, which are pure gold for month-on-month analysis. LAG(revenue, 1) OVER (ORDER BY month) fetches the previous row's revenue onto the current row, so you can compute growth without a self-join. LEAD peeks forward instead. The key intuition: the database sorts each partition, then for every row it can look backward, forward, or at neighbours — all in one pass, all without you writing a single JOIN.
lessons.example: Cars24 wants to rank sales executives within each city by cars sold this month, but keep every executive visible (not collapsed). In Gurugram, if Priya and Rahul both sold 18 cars, DENSE_RANK puts them both at rank 2 and the next person at rank 3 — fair and no gaps. Simultaneously they compute each executive's growth versus last month using LAG, all in one query over the sales table.
lessons.code:
-- Rank executives within each city, keep every row
SELECT
  city,
  executive_name,
  cars_sold,
  ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY city ORDER BY cars_sold DESC) AS rn,
  RANK()       OVER (PARTITION BY city ORDER BY cars_sold DESC) AS rnk,
  DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY city ORDER BY cars_sold DESC) AS drnk
FROM monthly_sales;

-- Month-on-month revenue growth with LAG (no self-join)
SELECT
  month,
  revenue,
  LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_revenue,
  revenue - LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS mom_change
FROM monthly_revenue
ORDER BY month;
lessons.tryit: Take any table with a category and a numeric value. Write one query that shows ROW_NUMBER, RANK, and DENSE_RANK side by side, PARTITIONed by category and ORDERed by the value descending. Deliberately create a tie and observe how the three columns differ on the tied rows and the row right after.

mistakes.m: Thinking window functions collapse rows like GROUP BY, then being confused when the output has the same number of rows as the input.
mistakes.fix: Remember windows ADD a column and preserve every row. If you actually want fewer rows, use GROUP BY, or wrap the window query in an outer query that filters (e.g. WHERE rn = 1).

mistakes.m: Using RANK when you meant DENSE_RANK (or vice versa) and getting unexpected gaps in the numbering after ties.
mistakes.fix: RANK skips after ties (1,2,2,4); DENSE_RANK does not (1,2,2,3); ROW_NUMBER never ties (1,2,3,4). Pick based on whether gaps after ties are acceptable for your report.

mistakes.m: Trying to filter on a window function directly in WHERE, e.g. WHERE ROW_NUMBER() OVER(...) = 1, which errors.
mistakes.fix: Window functions are computed after WHERE. Compute the window in a CTE or subquery, then filter on the alias in the outer query's WHERE.

quiz.q: Two funds tie for the 2nd highest AUM. Using RANK(), what rank does the very next (lower) fund get?
quiz.why: RANK() assigns the same rank to ties and then SKIPS the intervening numbers. Two funds tied at rank 2 occupy 'slots' 2 and 3, so the next fund gets rank 4. DENSE_RANK() would instead give it 3 because it never skips.
quiz.approach: When ties must not create gaps in your numbering (e.g. leaderboards where users expect 1,2,2,3), use DENSE_RANK. When you need a strict unique sequence, use ROW_NUMBER.

quiz.q: You want each row to also show the PREVIOUS month's revenue for a growth calculation. Which is the cleanest tool?
opts: A self-join on month = month-1
LAG(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month)
GROUP BY month with SUM
LEAD(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY month)
quiz.why: LAG fetches a value from an earlier row in the ordered partition directly onto the current row, avoiding a self-join entirely. LEAD would fetch the NEXT month, which is the wrong direction. GROUP BY collapses rows and can't reference neighbours.
quiz.approach: Reach for LAG/LEAD for period-over-period comparisons. They are faster to write, easier to read, and usually more efficient than self-joins.

quiz.q: Why does 'WHERE ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY x) = 1' fail?
opts: ROW_NUMBER needs PARTITION BY
Window functions are evaluated after WHERE, so WHERE can't reference them
ROW_NUMBER returns text, not a number
You must use RANK in a WHERE clause
quiz.why: In SQL's logical processing order, WHERE runs before SELECT and before window functions are computed. So the row number doesn't exist yet when WHERE is evaluated. You must compute it in a CTE/subquery first, then filter in an outer query.
quiz.approach: Whenever you need to filter on a window result (top-N-per-group), wrap the window in a CTE and filter the alias outside. This pattern is a workhorse.

lessons.title: The LEFT JOIN Filter Trap and Choosing the Right JOIN
lessons.concept:
Every JOIN type answers a different question, and the costliest bug in analytics is silently turning a LEFT JOIN into an INNER JOIN by accident. Start with the picture: INNER JOIN returns only rows that match in both tables. LEFT JOIN returns every row from the left table, and fills NULLs where the right table has no match. RIGHT JOIN is the mirror (rarely used — people just flip the table order). FULL OUTER JOIN keeps unmatched rows from both sides. The whole point of a LEFT JOIN is usually 'show me all my customers, INCLUDING the ones who never bought anything', where those non-buyers appear with NULL order details.

Here is the trap that catches even experienced people. You write a LEFT JOIN from customers to orders to keep everyone, then in the WHERE clause you add 'AND orders.status = "completed"'. Boom — you just silently deleted every customer who never ordered. Why? Because for a customer with no orders, orders.status is NULL, and NULL = 'completed' is not true, so WHERE throws that row away. Your LEFT JOIN has quietly become an INNER JOIN. The fix is to move the condition on the right-hand table from the WHERE clause INTO the ON clause: 'LEFT JOIN orders ON customers.id = orders.customer_id AND orders.status = "completed"'. Conditions in ON are applied WHILE matching (before NULLs are manufactured); conditions in WHERE are applied AFTER the join has already added NULL rows.

The rule to burn into memory: a filter on the LEFT (preserved) table can live in WHERE safely. A filter on the RIGHT (nullable) table must go in ON if you want to keep the unmatched left rows. If you genuinely want to keep only matched rows, then a WHERE filter is fine — but then you should probably have written an INNER JOIN and made your intent explicit. The only WHERE condition on the right table that is legitimate and powerful is 'WHERE orders.id IS NULL', which deliberately keeps ONLY the unmatched rows — the anti-join, perfect for 'find customers who never ordered'.
lessons.example: A fintech like Groww wants a report of ALL registered users and their completed SIP count, including users with zero completed SIPs so the growth team can re-engage them. An analyst writes a LEFT JOIN from users to sips but puts 'sips.status = "success"' in WHERE. The report suddenly shows only active investors — the 40,000 dormant users vanished. Moving that condition into the ON clause restores them (with a count of 0), and the re-engagement campaign is saved.
lessons.code:
-- WRONG: LEFT JOIN silently becomes INNER JOIN
SELECT u.user_id, COUNT(s.sip_id) AS success_sips
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN sips s ON u.user_id = s.user_id
WHERE s.status = 'success'      -- kills users with no matching sip
GROUP BY u.user_id;

-- RIGHT: condition on the nullable table goes in ON
SELECT u.user_id, COUNT(s.sip_id) AS success_sips
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN sips s
  ON u.user_id = s.user_id
 AND s.status = 'success'       -- filters during the match
GROUP BY u.user_id;             -- dormant users now show 0

-- ANTI-JOIN: users who never had a successful sip
SELECT u.user_id
FROM users u
LEFT JOIN sips s
  ON u.user_id = s.user_id AND s.status = 'success'
WHERE s.sip_id IS NULL;
lessons.tryit: Build a tiny customers table and orders table where at least one customer has no orders. Run a LEFT JOIN with a status filter first in WHERE, then move that same filter into ON. Count the rows both times and confirm the WHERE version drops your no-order customer while the ON version keeps them.

mistakes.m: Putting a filter on the right-hand (nullable) table in the WHERE clause of a LEFT JOIN and wondering why unmatched rows disappeared.
mistakes.fix: Move any condition on the right table into the ON clause. WHERE runs after NULLs are added, so 'right.col = value' silently discards the NULL (unmatched) rows.

mistakes.m: Assuming COUNT(*) and COUNT(right_table.column) give the same result in a LEFT JOIN.
mistakes.fix: COUNT(*) counts rows including the NULL-filled unmatched ones (so a no-order customer counts as 1); COUNT(orders.id) skips NULLs (counts as 0). Use COUNT(right.col) when you want '0 for non-matches'.

mistakes.m: Using RIGHT JOIN and confusing yourself about which table is preserved.
mistakes.fix: Prefer LEFT JOIN and just order your tables so the one you want to fully keep is on the left. It reads left-to-right and reduces mistakes.

quiz.q: You LEFT JOIN customers to orders to keep all customers, then add 'WHERE orders.region = "North"'. What happens to customers with no orders?
opts: They stay, with NULL order fields
They are dropped from the result
They appear twice
They are counted but hidden
quiz.why: For a customer with no order, orders.region is NULL. The comparison NULL = 'North' is not true, so WHERE removes that row. The LEFT JOIN has effectively become an INNER JOIN. To keep those customers, put the region condition in the ON clause instead.
quiz.approach: Any filter on the nullable side of a LEFT JOIN belongs in ON, not WHERE — unless you intentionally want an inner-join effect, in which case write INNER JOIN explicitly.

quiz.q: Which query correctly finds customers who have NEVER placed an order?
opts: INNER JOIN orders ON ... WHERE orders.id IS NULL
LEFT JOIN orders ON ... WHERE orders.id IS NULL
LEFT JOIN orders ON ... WHERE orders.id IS NOT NULL
RIGHT JOIN orders ON ... WHERE customers.id IS NULL
quiz.why: LEFT JOIN keeps all customers and fills NULLs where no order matched. Filtering WHERE orders.id IS NULL then keeps exactly the unmatched customers — the anti-join pattern. An INNER JOIN can never produce a NULL order id, so option 1 returns nothing.
quiz.approach: The LEFT JOIN + 'WHERE right_key IS NULL' idiom is the canonical way to find 'rows in A with no match in B'. Memorise it.

quiz.q: In a LEFT JOIN of customers to orders, which counts a no-order customer as 0 rather than 1?
opts: COUNT(*)
COUNT(1)
COUNT(orders.order_id)
COUNT(customers.customer_id)
quiz.why: COUNT ignores NULLs when given a specific column. For an unmatched customer, orders.order_id is NULL, so COUNT(orders.order_id) yields 0. COUNT(*), COUNT(1), and COUNT(customers.customer_id) all count the single NULL-filled row as 1.
quiz.approach: To count actual matches per group in an outer join, always COUNT a non-null column from the RIGHT table, never COUNT(*).

lessons.title: CTEs, Subqueries, and EXISTS: Structuring Thought in SQL
lessons.concept:
As queries grow, the difference between a senior and a junior is structure. A CTE (Common Table Expression) — the WITH clause — lets you name a query result and reuse it, turning a tangled nested mess into a readable top-to-bottom pipeline. 'WITH high_value AS (SELECT ...) SELECT ... FROM high_value' reads like a paragraph: define your building block, then use it. CTEs can chain (one CTE referencing an earlier one) and can even be recursive for hierarchies like org charts or category trees. Crucially, a CTE is about readability and reuse; in most modern databases it is not automatically faster than a subquery, so use it to think clearly, not as a performance trick.

Subqueries come in flavours you must distinguish. A scalar subquery returns one value and can sit in SELECT or WHERE ('WHERE price > (SELECT AVG(price) FROM products)'). An IN subquery returns a list to match against. A correlated subquery references the outer query and re-runs conceptually for each outer row. This is where EXISTS shines. EXISTS just asks 'does at least one matching row exist?' and stops at the first hit — it does not care about values, only existence. 'WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.id)' means 'customers who have at least one order'.

The subtle but exam-critical point is EXISTS versus IN when NULLs are involved, specifically NOT IN. NOT EXISTS behaves predictably. But NOT IN is a landmine: if the subquery's list contains even a single NULL, 'x NOT IN (list with NULL)' evaluates to UNKNOWN for every x, and the whole query returns ZERO rows — silently. This has burned countless analysts debugging why 'customers not in the blacklist' returned nothing. The professional habit: prefer NOT EXISTS for anti-membership tests, or guarantee the subquery column is NOT NULL. EXISTS is also often more efficient because it can short-circuit on the first match rather than building a full list.
lessons.example: A lending startup like KreditBee wants applicants who have NO prior defaulted loan. An analyst writes 'WHERE applicant_id NOT IN (SELECT applicant_id FROM defaults)'. But the defaults table has a few rows where applicant_id is NULL (bad data), so NOT IN returns UNKNOWN everywhere and the eligibility list comes back completely empty — halting disbursals. Rewriting with NOT EXISTS (correlated on applicant_id) ignores the NULL problem entirely and returns the correct list of clean applicants.
lessons.code:
-- Readable pipeline with a CTE
WITH monthly AS (
  SELECT customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date) AS mth, SUM(amount) AS spend
  FROM orders
  GROUP BY customer_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', order_date)
)
SELECT customer_id, AVG(spend) AS avg_monthly_spend
FROM monthly
GROUP BY customer_id;

-- EXISTS: customers with at least one order (short-circuits)
SELECT c.customer_id, c.name
FROM customers c
WHERE EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id
);

-- SAFE anti-membership: NOT EXISTS ignores NULL trouble
SELECT a.applicant_id
FROM applicants a
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1 FROM defaults d WHERE d.applicant_id = a.applicant_id
);
-- Compare: NOT IN (SELECT applicant_id FROM defaults) returns 0 rows if any is NULL
lessons.tryit: Create a defaults table containing one NULL applicant_id. Run 'SELECT ... WHERE applicant_id NOT IN (SELECT applicant_id FROM defaults)' and watch it return zero rows. Then rewrite with NOT EXISTS and confirm you get the correct non-defaulters. This one experiment will make the NULL trap unforgettable.

mistakes.m: Using NOT IN with a subquery whose column can contain NULL, then getting an empty result set with no error.
mistakes.fix: Prefer NOT EXISTS for anti-membership, or add 'WHERE col IS NOT NULL' inside the subquery. NOT IN with any NULL in the list makes every comparison UNKNOWN, filtering out everything.

mistakes.m: Believing a CTE is automatically faster than the equivalent subquery.
mistakes.fix: CTEs are primarily for readability and reuse. In many engines they optimise identically to subqueries (and older Postgres even materialised them as an optimisation fence). Use them for clarity; measure if performance matters.

mistakes.m: Selecting real columns inside an EXISTS subquery and worrying about what they return.
mistakes.fix: EXISTS only checks for the existence of any row; the SELECT list is irrelevant. Write SELECT 1 to signal intent — the database never uses the projected values.

quiz.q: Your 'WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT blocked_id FROM blocklist)' returns zero rows even though most ids aren't blocked. Most likely cause?
opts: blocklist is empty
blocked_id contains at least one NULL
You need DISTINCT in the subquery
NOT IN isn't valid SQL
quiz.why: If the subquery returns any NULL, then for every outer id the expression 'id NOT IN (…, NULL, …)' evaluates to UNKNOWN (never TRUE), so no row passes the filter. This is the classic NOT IN + NULL trap. NOT EXISTS avoids it because it tests row existence, not value equality.
quiz.approach: Default to NOT EXISTS for 'not present in the other table' logic. If you must use NOT IN, guarantee the inner column is NOT NULL.

quiz.q: What does EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM orders o WHERE o.cust_id = c.id) actually evaluate?
opts: The sum of order amounts
Whether at least one matching order row exists
The literal value 1 for each order
The number of orders as an integer
quiz.why: EXISTS is a boolean test that returns TRUE as soon as the subquery produces at least one row, ignoring the projected columns entirely. That's why SELECT 1 is idiomatic — the value is never used; only the presence or absence of rows matters.
quiz.approach: Use EXISTS for existence checks; it can short-circuit on the first match and is often more efficient than counting or building an IN list.

quiz.q: What is the primary reason to use a CTE (WITH clause)?
opts: It always runs faster than a subquery
It readably names and reuses a result, structuring the query as a pipeline
It is the only way to do aggregation
It bypasses the WHERE clause
quiz.why: CTEs exist mainly to improve readability and let you reference a named intermediate result (even multiple times, or recursively). They are not a guaranteed performance optimisation — in many databases they plan the same as an equivalent subquery.
quiz.approach: Use CTEs to make complex logic legible and maintainable. Chain them to build a clear step-by-step pipeline; don't assume they speed things up.

lessons.title: Logical Order, Star Schema, and Writing Queries That Scale
lessons.concept:
To master SQL you must know the order the database THINKS in, which is different from the order you WRITE in. You write SELECT ... FROM ... WHERE ... GROUP BY ... HAVING ... ORDER BY. But the engine executes FROM/JOIN first, then WHERE, then GROUP BY, then HAVING, then SELECT (including window functions and column aliases), then ORDER BY, and finally LIMIT. This single fact explains a dozen 'why doesn't this work' mysteries. Why can't you use a SELECT alias in WHERE? Because WHERE runs before SELECT computes the alias. Why does WHERE filter raw rows but HAVING filter groups? Because WHERE runs before grouping (on individual rows) and HAVING runs after (on aggregated groups). Use WHERE to cut rows early — it is cheaper because it shrinks the data before the expensive grouping.

This order also drives optimisation intuition. The golden rule is filter early and filter cheaply. Push conditions into WHERE so fewer rows reach the GROUP BY and JOINs. Avoid wrapping an indexed column in a function in the WHERE clause — 'WHERE YEAR(order_date) = 2026' forces the engine to compute YEAR on every row and usually cannot use an index on order_date; rewrite it as a sargable range 'WHERE order_date >= "2026-01-01" AND order_date < "2027-01-01"' so the index does the work. Avoid SELECT * in production queries (you drag unnecessary columns across the network and defeat covering indexes). Prefer UNION ALL over UNION when you know rows are already distinct, because UNION silently adds a full de-duplication sort that UNION ALL skips.

Finally, analytics data is usually organised as a star schema, and understanding it makes your queries faster and clearer. At the centre sits a large fact table (one row per event — a sale, a click, a payment) holding foreign keys and numeric measures. Around it sit small dimension tables (date, product, customer, store) holding descriptive attributes. You join the fact to the dimensions on keys, filter and group by dimension attributes, and aggregate the fact's measures. This design keeps the huge fact table narrow and the descriptive text in small lookup tables — the pattern behind virtually every BI dashboard in Power BI or Tableau you'll ever build.
lessons.example: At a retailer like DMart, the fact_sales table has 500 million rows: date_key, store_key, product_key, quantity, amount. To answer 'total revenue of dairy products in Maharashtra stores for Q1 FY26', you join fact_sales to dim_product (to filter category = 'Dairy'), dim_store (state = 'Maharashtra'), and dim_date (quarter = 'Q1'), then SUM(amount). Filtering on the small dimensions and aggregating the fact is exactly what star schemas are built for — and writing the date filter as a range instead of YEAR(date_key) lets the index scan only three months instead of the whole table.
lessons.code:
-- Logical order in action: WHERE filters rows, HAVING filters groups
SELECT store_key, SUM(amount) AS revenue
FROM fact_sales
WHERE sale_date >= '2025-04-01' AND sale_date < '2025-07-01'  -- sargable, uses index
GROUP BY store_key
HAVING SUM(amount) > 1000000     -- filter AFTER aggregation
ORDER BY revenue DESC
LIMIT 10;

-- Star schema join: fact aggregated, filtered via small dimensions
SELECT d.quarter, p.category, SUM(f.amount) AS revenue
FROM fact_sales f
JOIN dim_date    d ON f.date_key    = d.date_key
JOIN dim_product p ON f.product_key = p.product_key
JOIN dim_store   s ON f.store_key   = s.store_key
WHERE s.state = 'Maharashtra' AND p.category = 'Dairy'
GROUP BY d.quarter, p.category;

-- UNION ALL (fast) vs UNION (adds a de-dup sort)
SELECT city FROM online_orders
UNION ALL                        -- keeps duplicates, no sort
SELECT city FROM store_orders;
lessons.tryit: Take a slow-ish date filter written as WHERE YEAR(order_date) = 2026 and rewrite it as a half-open range WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01' AND order_date < '2027-01-01'. If you have EXPLAIN available, compare the query plans and see whether the rewrite switches from a full scan to an index range scan.

mistakes.m: Putting an aggregate condition like SUM(amount) > 100000 in WHERE instead of HAVING.
mistakes.fix: WHERE runs before grouping and can't see aggregates. Filter individual rows in WHERE; filter aggregated groups in HAVING. Using WHERE for row-level conditions also speeds things up by shrinking data before GROUP BY.

mistakes.m: Wrapping an indexed column in a function in WHERE, e.g. YEAR(order_date) = 2026 or UPPER(name) = 'RAHUL', killing index usage.
mistakes.fix: Keep the column bare and transform the constant side instead: use a date range for years, and store/compare a normalised column for case-insensitive search. This keeps the predicate 'sargable' so indexes apply.

mistakes.m: Using UNION everywhere out of habit, paying for a de-duplication sort you don't need.
mistakes.fix: Use UNION ALL when duplicates are impossible or acceptable — it simply concatenates and is much faster. Reserve UNION for when you genuinely must remove duplicate rows across the two sets.

quiz.q: Why can't you reference a column alias defined in SELECT inside the WHERE clause of the same query?
opts: Aliases are only for display
WHERE is logically executed before SELECT computes the alias
You must use single quotes on aliases
It works in all databases actually
quiz.why: The logical processing order is FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY. Since WHERE runs before SELECT, the alias doesn't exist yet when WHERE is evaluated. ORDER BY, which runs after SELECT, CAN use the alias. To filter on a computed expression, repeat the expression in WHERE or wrap it in a subquery/CTE.
quiz.approach: Internalise the logical order — it explains alias scope, WHERE vs HAVING, and where window functions become available.

quiz.q: Which WHERE clause on an indexed order_date column is 'sargable' (able to use the index) for the year 2026?
opts: WHERE YEAR(order_date) = 2026
WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01' AND order_date < '2027-01-01'
WHERE CAST(order_date AS CHAR) LIKE '2026%'
WHERE DATEPART(year, order_date) = 2026
quiz.why: Wrapping the column in a function (YEAR, CAST, DATEPART) forces the engine to evaluate it row-by-row and generally prevents using an index on order_date. A half-open range leaves the column bare, so the index can seek directly to the 2026 rows. It's also correct across leap years and timestamps.
quiz.approach: Keep indexed columns unwrapped in predicates; transform the constant instead. Prefer half-open date ranges [start, end) for period filters.

quiz.q: In a star schema, where do the numeric measures you SUM (like revenue) live, and where do descriptive filters (like category) live?
opts: Measures in dimensions; filters in the fact
Measures in the fact table; descriptive attributes in dimension tables
Both in one big flat table
Measures in a CTE; filters in a view
quiz.why: A star schema centres a large fact table holding foreign keys plus numeric measures (quantity, amount), surrounded by small dimension tables holding descriptive attributes (product category, store state, date parts). You filter/group by dimension attributes and aggregate the fact's measures — keeping the huge fact table narrow and lookups small.
quiz.approach: Model analytics as fact + dimensions: narrow fact for events/measures, rich dimensions for descriptions. Filter on dimensions, aggregate the fact.
